questions.scenario: A laptop has no cached answer for `api.example.com`. Its local resolver sends the query to `10.0.0.53`, which follows referrals until `ns1.dns-provider.example` returns an A record. Which role assignment is accurate?

options.text: The laptop is authoritative for `example.com`, while `10.0.0.53` is the application server named by the A record
The laptop uses a stub resolver, `10.0.0.53` performs recursion for the client, and the provider nameserver is authoritative for the zone
The root nameserver returns the final application IP directly, while the provider nameserver only caches it
The TLD nameserver terminates HTTPS, while the recursive resolver chooses the service's TCP port

questions.explanation: The local stub asks a recursive resolver to do the lookup. If the answer is not cached, that resolver follows root and TLD referrals to the authoritative nameserver that owns the final record. Root and TLD servers delegate; they do not terminate the application connection. [RFC 1034](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1034) describes DNS hierarchy, delegation, and resolver roles.

questions.scenario: A retailer is moving its web front door to `203.0.113.25`. The platform team wants `app.example.com` to publish that IPv4 address, while `www.example.com` must follow future changes to the app hostname without a second address update. Existing mail delivery for `example.com` cannot change. Which DNS change best meets the migration requirement?

options.text: Create separate A records for `app` and `www` with the same address, then update both during every later migration
Create an A record for `www` and a CNAME from `app` to `www`, making the app name depend on the secondary alias
Replace the domain's MX record with a CNAME to `app` so web and mail traffic follow one target
Create an A record for `app` and a CNAME from `www` to `app`, leaving the domain's MX records alone

questions.explanation: An A record gives `app` the required IPv4 address, and the CNAME makes `www` follow later app target changes. Two A records would work initially but create two migration controls, while reversing the alias makes the primary app name depend on `www`. Changing MX would violate the mail constraint. [RFC 1035](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1035) defines these record roles.

questions.scenario:
A corporate resolver and an authoritative nameserver disagree:

```text
$ dig @10.0.0.53 api.example.com A
;; ->>HEADER<<- opcode: QUERY, status: SERVFAIL, id: 39011

$ dig @ns1.dns-provider.example api.example.com A
;; ->>HEADER<<- opcode: QUERY, status: NOERROR, id: 8821
;; flags: qr aa; QUERY: 1, ANSWER: 1
;; ANSWER SECTION:
api.example.com. 300 IN A 203.0.113.25
```

What should be investigated first?

options.text: The recursive resolver's validation or upstream path, because the authoritative server can return a valid answer
The application listener on port 443, because SERVFAIL is an HTTP response from the origin
A missing hostname, because SERVFAIL and NXDOMAIN both prove the record does not exist
The A record's TTL, because any positive TTL prevents authoritative answers from being returned

questions.explanation: The `aa` flag and NOERROR response show that the authoritative server has the record. SERVFAIL means the recursive resolver could not complete its lookup, often due to validation, delegation, or upstream reachability. NXDOMAIN would explicitly mean the name does not exist, and HTTP has not started. The [BIND `dig` manual](https://bind9.readthedocs.io/en/latest/manpages.html#dig-query-dns-lookup-utility) documents these diagnostic fields.

questions.scenario: `api.example.com` has a TTL of 3,600 seconds. The team lowers it to 60 seconds five minutes before changing the load balancer address. After the cutover, some customers still reach the old address with about 3,200 seconds remaining in their resolver cache. Which planning change would have prevented most of this split traffic?

options.text: Set the TTL to zero at cutover time because existing cache entries immediately adopt the new value
Replace the A record with an MX record so recursive resolvers stop caching the web destination
Lower the TTL at least one old-TTL period before the address change, then keep the old target healthy while caches expire
Change the authoritative nameservers at the same time so cached A records become invalid

questions.explanation: Resolvers that cached the record before the TTL change continue honoring the old 3,600-second lifetime. Lowering the TTL early and waiting one old-TTL window lets caches learn the shorter value before the cutover. Record type changes or delegation churn add risk and do not erase existing caches. [AWS Route 53 DNS best practices](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/Route53/latest/DeveloperGuide/best-practices-dns.html) covers TTL planning and resilient DNS changes.

questions.scenario: Employees on the company VPN reach `app.example.com` at `10.20.5.15`, while a public resolver returns `203.0.113.25`. Both paths are intentionally deployed. A remote employee disconnects from VPN and starts receiving the public address. What is the most accurate explanation?

options.text: The public resolver is corrupt because one hostname is allowed to have only one answer worldwide
Split-horizon DNS is returning different valid answers based on the resolver path, so the failing client's configured resolver must be identified
The private address is a CNAME that browsers automatically convert into a public IP when VPN disconnects
The TTL determines whether an address is private or public, so increasing it will force the private route

questions.explanation: Private and public zones can deliberately provide different answers for the same hostname. The key incident fact is which resolver the client used and whether that resolver is associated with the intended private zone. TTL affects cache lifetime, not address scope, and a CNAME does not translate private addresses into public ones. [RFC 1034](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1034) provides the underlying namespace and resolver model.

questions.scenario:
A release engineer compares DNS evidence after a blue-green cutover. Which observations are valid? Select all that apply.

```text
$ dig @1.1.1.1 api.example.com A
;; ->>HEADER<<- opcode: QUERY, status: NOERROR, id: 1010
;; ANSWER SECTION:
api.example.com. 60 IN A 203.0.113.99
;; SERVER: 1.1.1.1#53(1.1.1.1) (UDP)

$ dig @10.0.0.53 api.example.com A
;; ->>HEADER<<- opcode: QUERY, status: NOERROR, id: 2020
;; ANSWER SECTION:
api.example.com. 2418 IN A 203.0.113.34
;; SERVER: 10.0.0.53#53(10.0.0.53) (UDP)
```

options.text: Both resolvers agree because both responses have status NOERROR
The `SERVER` lines prove that different recursive resolver paths answered the two queries
The larger TTL proves `203.0.113.34` is the newer authoritative value
The internal resolver may continue serving the old address until its remaining TTL expires
The old target should remain healthy because clients using `10.0.0.53` can still be sent there

questions.explanation: NOERROR means each lookup succeeded, not that the answers match. The resolver identities and A records differ, and the remaining TTL shows how long the old cached answer may persist. A higher TTL does not make an answer newer. Keeping both targets healthy protects clients while caches converge. [RFC 1035](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1035) defines TTL and answer semantics.

questions.scenario: A new branch office cannot resolve `app.example.com`, although the same name resolves from headquarters and from the authoritative server. A branch workstation receives `connection timed out; no servers could be reached` when it queries its configured resolver at `10.40.0.53`. The company must restore name resolution without changing a healthy public zone. What should the network team investigate first?

options.text: The branch path and policy to `10.40.0.53`, because the client received no DNS response while authoritative data works elsewhere
The public A record, because a client timeout proves the authoritative server returned NXDOMAIN
The record TTL, because lowering cache lifetime makes an unreachable resolver answer more quickly
The application listener, because DNS timeout occurs only after the client has completed TCP and TLS to the web server

questions.explanation: The branch received no response from its configured resolver, while other clients and the authoritative source can resolve the name. That constrains the first investigation to resolver reachability or branch policy. Changing the record or TTL would not repair that path, and the web listener is reached only after DNS succeeds. [RFC 1035](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1035) distinguishes DNS responses from transport timeouts.

questions.scenario:
A dual-stack launch adds this zone data:

```text
app.example.com. 300 IN A    203.0.113.25
app.example.com. 300 IN AAAA 2001:db8:10::25
www.example.com. 300 IN CNAME app.example.com.
```

IPv4 checks pass, but IPv6 users fail to connect to `www.example.com`. Which next check follows from the records?

options.text: Replace the CNAME with an MX record because aliases cannot lead to IPv6 addresses
Delete the A record because resolvers cannot return A and AAAA records for the same owner
Resolve the CNAME target's AAAA answer and test the IPv6 network path, since the alias can lead clients to the separate IPv6 destination
Increase the TXT record TTL because TXT data controls which address family browsers choose

questions.explanation: The CNAME points `www` to `app`, and the target publishes both IPv4 and IPv6 addresses. IPv4 success does not validate the IPv6 route, listener, or firewall. MX and TXT records solve different problems, and dual-stack owners can publish both A and AAAA records. [RFC 1035](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1035) defines aliases and address records.
